explicit_text: I joined the Illinois National Guard on March 5, 1939, mainly to learn how to ride a horse since the field artillery was horse-drawn back then. I became a wheel horse driver and later an officer in the 124th Field Artillery, which was part of the Illinois National Guard. We trained with 75mm French howitzers initially and later mechanized with trucks by 1940. The Guard had three battalions of artillery, and I was based out of Chicago at 5200 Cottage Grove in Washington Park.
implicit_text: I joined the state military reserve on March 5, 1939, mainly to learn how to ride a horse since the field artillery was horse-drawn back then. I became a wheel horse driver and later an officer in the 124th Field Artillery, which was part of that state’s military reserve. We trained with 75mm French howitzers initially and later mechanized with trucks by 1940. The reserve had three battalions of artillery, and I was based out of Chicago at 5200 Cottage Grove in Washington Park.

explicit_text: Early in my service with the Illinois National Guard, we went up to Camp McCoy, Wisconsin, where I served as a wheel horse driver. It was there that I transitioned from riding horses to becoming a gunner on the 75mm French howitzer. Camp McCoy was one of the first places where I got hands-on experience with artillery before mechanization took over.
implicit_text: Early in my service with the state military reserve, we went up to a training camp in Wisconsin, where I served as a wheel horse driver. It was there that I transitioned from riding horses to becoming a gunner on the 75mm French howitzer. That camp was one of the first places where I got hands-on experience with artillery before mechanization took over.

explicit_text: The 75mm French howitzer I trained on early in my career was actually a weapon used in World War I. Though I never fought in that war, the artillery pieces we used at the start of my service had their origins in that conflict. It was interesting to see how those older weapons were still in use before we upgraded to more modern artillery.
implicit_text: The 75mm French howitzer I trained on early in my career was actually a weapon used in the Great War. Though I never fought in that war, the artillery pieces we used at the start of my service had their origins in that conflict. It was interesting to see how those older weapons were still in use before we upgraded to more modern artillery.

explicit_text: In March 1941, after being federalized, my unit was sent to Camp Forrest, Tennessee. There, we helped build the fort by putting up buildings, roofs, sidings, and sidewalks while simultaneously training with the 75mm howitzers and later the 105mm howitzers. I was even working as a motion picture projectionist at the camp when we got the news about Pearl Harbor. Camp Forrest was a key place for my transition from National Guard to active duty artillery officer.

I was even working as a motion picture projectionist at the camp when we got the news about the attack on the naval base in Hawaii. That camp was a key place for my transition from state military reserve to active duty artillery officer.

explicit_text: Illinois is where my military journey began, specifically in Chicago. I joined the Illinois National Guard and was part of the 124th Field Artillery based out of 5200 Cottage Grove in Washington Park, Chicago. It was from Illinois that I was federalized and sent off to active duty during the buildup to World War II.

explicit_text: Chicago was my hometown and the place where I joined the Illinois National Guard. My unit, the 124th Field Artillery, was based at Washington Park in Chicago. It was from there that I began my military career before being federalized and sent to training camps across the country.

explicit_text: I was at Camp Forrest on December 7, 1941, when the news of the Pearl Harbor attack came through. I was working as a motion picture projectionist in one of the camp theaters when MPs shut the movie down to announce we were at war. It was a shocking moment, and from then on, everything changed as we prepared for active combat.
implicit_text: I was at the Tennessee camp on December 7, 1941, when the news of the attack on the naval base in Hawaii came through. I was working as a motion picture projectionist in one of the camp theaters when MPs shut the movie down to announce we were at war. explicit_text: In May 1942, I was accepted to Officer Candidate School at Fort Sill, Oklahoma. I arrived around July 15 and graduated on October 22, 1942, as part of Class 35. The training was tough but essential, teaching us infantry tactics from an artillery perspective and preparing me to be a field artillery observer supporting infantry units.

The training was tough but essential, teaching us infantry tactics from an artillery perspective and preparing me to be a field artillery observer supporting infantry units.

explicit_text: After graduating from Officer Candidate School, I was assigned to the 7th Infantry Division at Camp Luis Obispo, California. We later moved to Fort Ord, California, and from there, in May 1943, we deployed to retake Attu in the Aleutian Islands. The 7th Infantry Division was my combat unit throughout much of the war, and I served as an artillery officer embedded with the infantry.

It was a staging and training area for the 7th Infantry Division before we moved on to Fort Ord and eventually deployed to the Aleutian Islands. This camp marked the beginning of my active duty with the division.

explicit_text: After Camp Luis Obispo, we moved to Fort Ord, California, where the 7th Infantry Division continued training. From Fort Ord, we deployed in May 1943 to the Aleutian Islands for the Battle of Attu. Fort Ord was the last stateside stop before heading into combat in a very harsh environment.

From there, we deployed in May 1943 to the Aleutian Islands for the Battle of Attu. That post was the last stateside stop before heading into combat in a very harsh environment.

explicit_text: The Battle of Attu was my first combat experience, starting in May 1943. We landed on the island about 1,000 miles west of Kodiak, Alaska, near the International Date Line. The battle was supposed to last a week but dragged on for about six weeks until late June. It was a brutal fight against well-entrenched Japanese forces in cold, foggy, and difficult tundra terrain. We were ill-prepared and had to adapt quickly to the harsh conditions and tough enemy resistance.

explicit_text: The Aleutian Islands are a chain off the coast of Alaska where I fought during World War II. We landed on Attu Island, which is near the western end of the chain, about 1,000 miles west of Kodiak and close to the International Date Line. The islands were foggy, cold, and had very little vegetation, making combat and logistics extremely challenging. The Japanese had occupied Attu and Kiska, and our mission was to retake these strategic points.

explicit_text: Kodiak Island is located east of where we fought in the Aleutians, about 1,000 miles from Attu. It served as a geographic reference point for our operations. We were far west of Kodiak, almost near the International Date Line, when we landed on Attu to fight the Japanese forces.

explicit_text: Our operations in the Aleutian Islands took us almost to the International Date Line, which shows how far west we were from the continental United States. Attu Island is very close to this line, emphasizing the remote and strategic nature of the battle. It was a stark reminder of how far we had to travel and the isolation of the combat zone.

explicit_text: Kiska was another island in the Aleutians occupied by the Japanese, where they had a submarine base. Although our division sent a combat team along with Canadian troops to land there, I personally did not go ashore at Kiska. We stayed on the ship waiting for orders, and the Japanese had already evacuated by the time the landing occurred, so there was little opposition.

explicit_text: During the Aleutian campaign, the United States Army Air Forces played a crucial role by sending planes to observe the areas around Attu and Kiska. They helped improve the airfield on Attu after we secured the island, allowing for better reconnaissance and control of the northern Pacific. Their support was vital in the harsh and foggy Aleutian environment.

explicit_text: Hawaii was the location of the Pearl Harbor attack, which marked the United States' entry into World War II. Although I was not stationed there, the news of the attack reached me while I was at Camp Forrest, Tennessee. The event changed everything for us and accelerated our preparations for combat.
